prompt: Please conduct a study and prepare a report on the 'Construction and Application of a Sports Intelligent Tutoring and Learning Guidance System Driven by Multimodal Data Fusion.'
article:
Assumptions used (to avoid underspecification): “Sports Intelligent Tutoring & Learning Guidance System” (S-ITS) is treated as an end-to-end system that (1) senses athlete/learner performance via multiple modalities, (2) fuses data to infer technique/tactics/loads/risks, (3) maintains a learner model, and (4) delivers *personalized guidance* (feedback + practice planning + reflection) in real time and/or after practice. “Driven by multimodal data fusion” means fusion is central to robustness and personalization, not an optional add-on.

---

## 1) What such a system is, and what “construction + application” concretely entails

A sports S-ITS can be decomposed into three layers:

1) **Measurement layer (multimodal sensing)**  
   Captures *how the learner moved* (kinematics), *what happened* (outcomes), *context* (opponents, ball, environment), and potentially *internal state* (physiology, fatigue, attention).

2) **Inference layer (multimodal fusion + models)**  
   Converts raw streams into interpretable events/metrics: pose, phases, joint angles, skill segments, tactical states, quality scores, workload, anomaly/injury signals, uncertainty.

3) **Tutoring/guidance layer (pedagogy + personalization + UX)**  
   Chooses *what to say/show/do next* (feedback type, timing, specificity, drills), aligned with motor learning principles and with the user’s level and goals.

This aligns with how the physical-movement learning pipeline is often described in pose-estimation-based systems: **pose estimation → movement assessment → augmented feedback** [[63]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11401083/).

---

## 2) Requirements, use-cases, and pedagogical goals (2022–2026)

### 2.1 Core requirements across settings
Across PE, club coaching, collegiate/elite, and rehab-like training, recurring requirements are:

- **Personalization**: avoid one-size-fits-all instruction; adapt to differences in physique, skill, and interest [[1]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/public-health/articles/10.3389/fpubh.2024.1484848/full).  
- **Closed-loop learning**: measure → analyze → guide → re-measure, continuously optimizing plans [[1]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/public-health/articles/10.3389/fpubh.2024.1484848/full).  
- **Real-time + post-session modes**: immediate correction when safe/appropriate; deeper review later.  
- **Interpretability & trust**: coaching staff often become “data analysts” without formal training, and must explain/defend metrics while protecting athlete wellbeing and privacy [[3]](https://learndialogue.org/pdf/MBrewerCHI2025.pdf).  
- **Responsible use of biometric/performance tech**: governed policies, consent, and education (e.g., NCAA work on responsible performance-technology guidance via Delphi-style consensus) [[4]](https://www.ncaa.org/news/2025/12/11/media-center-performance-technology-guidance-approved-by-csmas.aspx).  
- **Athlete-centered design**: IOC’s AI framing emphasizes humans remain central and AI should expand access while preserving self-determination and dignity [[6]](https://stillmed.olympics.com/media/Documents/International-Olympic-Committee/AI/Olympic-AI-Agenda-launch-President-speech.pdf).

### 2.2 Use-cases by context (and what “learning guidance” means in each)

#### (A) School Physical Education (PE) / mass instruction
Goals emphasized include skill acquisition, fitness, engagement, equity of instruction, and scalable evaluation [[1]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/public-health/articles/10.3389/fpubh.2024.1484848/full).  
System features that map well:

- Automated observation and evaluation of movements (objective rubrics) [[1]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/public-health/articles/10.3389/fpubh.2024.1484848/full).
- Personalized learning plans based on measured performance [[1]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/public-health/articles/10.3389/fpubh.2024.1484848/full).
- Engagement via VR/AR or gamified elements (often cited as motivational levers) [[1]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/public-health/articles/10.3389/fpubh.2024.1484848/full).

Evidence base in PE indicates **visual feedback and visual+verbal feedback outperform verbal-only** for motor skill learning in PE contexts [[89]](https://www.mdpi.com/1660-4601/19/22/15361), and video-based visual feedback appears effective and more effective than solely verbal feedback, while deployment constraints (class size, time, equipment, data protection) must be addressed [[90]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s12662-021-00782-y).

#### (B) Club coaching / individual skill training (technique-centric)
Typical goals: form correction, consistency, deliberate practice structure, self-training support.

A representative design pattern is “AI coach compares learner vs expert motion and visualizes differences/recommendations” [[13]](https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/3582700.3582710), [[14]](https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/AI-Coach%3A-A-Motor-Skill-Training-System-using-Liao-Hwang/22a26f41d84acb826f43a3d887c45b2ec7b5895a).  
Multimodal fusion matters because camera-only pose can fail under occlusion or view changes, while IMU-only can drift or lose global context.

#### (C) Team sports tactical tutoring / decision-making
Goals shift from “form only” to **perception–cognition–action** and multi-agent context.

A concrete example: an AI-assisted soccer/football tactics instruction model combining **ChatGPT language guidance + visualization** produced significantly greater improvements than traditional instruction in tactical comprehension, tactical decision-making, and learning satisfaction in male college students [[2]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001691825007449).  
So “multimodal” here includes *language* + *visual analytics*, not just sensors.

#### (D) Collegiate / elite performance monitoring and staff workflows
Goals: performance optimization, load management, injury risk reduction—within high-stakes constraints.

Collegiate coaching staff report GPS trackers, wearables, and movement analysis systems have become “standard”; they operate across a **data life cycle** (gathering → sharing → deciding → acting → assessing) while balancing performance aims with privacy and emotional wellbeing [[3]](https://learndialogue.org/pdf/MBrewerCHI2025.pdf).  
Implication: S-ITS must support *organizational workflows*, not only athlete-facing feedback.

#### (E) Safety/welfare protocols (cross-sport, especially elite)
AI opportunities include real-time safety monitoring and policy support. For example, elite rugby’s **instrumented mouthguards** providing real-time head-impact signals are discussed as an AI-enabled safeguard complementing concussion tools [[5]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11503112/).  
This pushes S-ITS designs toward **risk-aware, uncertainty-aware outputs** and stringent governance [[5]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11503112/).

---

## 3) Practical multimodal data sources (2022–2026) and what they’re good for

### 3.1 Modalities and the tutoring-relevant signals they provide

**RGB video (single or multi-view)**
- Strengths: ubiquitous capture (phones), intuitive review, supports pose estimation and action recognition.  
- Weaknesses: occlusion, lighting, motion blur, viewpoint dependence (especially in sports) [[28]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s10462-024-10934-9), [[36]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s10462-025-11344-1).

**Depth / RGB-D / stereo**
- Strengths: adds 3D geometry; can improve 3D pose robustness. Commercial options include Kinect, ZED, RealSense (used in sports/exercise markerless analysis literature) [[29]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12378739/).  
- Weaknesses: cost, outdoor performance and setup constraints, still affected by occlusion [[28]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s10462-024-10934-9), [[29]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12378739/).

**Markerless pose estimation frameworks (software stack)**
- Widely referenced open-source toolkits include OpenPose and MediaPipe [[29]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12378739/), with mobile-friendly pose estimation highlighted in mobile deployments [[37]](https://excel.fit.vutbr.cz/submissions/2024/073/73.pdf). MMPose is a widely used toolbox/benchmark ecosystem for pose estimation models [[39]](https://github.com/open-mmlab/mmpose).

**IMU wearables (accelerometer/gyroscope, sometimes magnetometer)**
- Strengths: portable, works outside lab, high-rate signals; supports phase detection and joint kinematics with calibration.  
- Weaknesses: sensor-to-segment alignment/calibration is difficult; accuracy varies by joint/task; sports dynamics add noise, impacts, skin artifact [[30]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10534374/), and alignment errors can rapidly degrade joint angles [[31]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s12283-024-00483-3).

**GNSS/GPS athlete tracking (mostly team sports)**
- Strengths: field-scale locomotor load, speed/acceleration patterns (useful in workload/injury models).  
- Weaknesses: accuracy and acceleration derivation depend on sampling/filtering; vendor claims exist but are not always independently validated [[52]](https://www.catapult.com/blog/sample-rate-satellite-athlete-tracking-technologies).

**Force plates**
- Strengths: gold-standard kinetics (GRF, COP), jump/landing quality, balance tasks; excellent for validation and for some feedback loops.  
- Weaknesses: lab-bound cost/setup; limits scale [[33]](https://physionet.org/content/olst-mocap-forceplate-radar/).

**Pressure insoles**
- Strengths: in-field foot loading patterns; useful for running/gait retraining and technique cues.  
- Practical constraint: sampling vs battery. Downsampling from 100 Hz to **20 Hz kept peak-force error <3%** on average in a running study, while 10 Hz increased error to ~9% [[32]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0021929022004286).

**Surface EMG**
- Strengths: muscle activation timing/intensity, useful for coaching cues (activation patterns) and fatigue analysis.  
- Requirements: strict electrode placement (SENIAM), skin prep, high sampling (e.g., 1500 Hz used in reliability testing) [[47]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11297106/), [[49]](http://www.seniam.org/). Some EMG-derived measures are less reliable than mean amplitude [[47]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11297106/).

**Audio**
- Often paired with video; can support event cues (impact, cadence) and is explicitly listed as a possible HAR modality [[28]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s10462-024-10934-9).

**Eye tracking**
- Captures gaze strategies underlying expertise and decision-making. Wearable eye tracking has been demonstrated in naturalistic table tennis tasks and yields group differences in gaze metrics such as fixation frequency and pupil diameter [[34]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0167945725001307).  
- For tutoring: enables “where should you look” guidance, not just “how you moved.”

---

## 4) Acquisition, synchronization, calibration, labeling, and quality control (QC)

### 4.1 Synchronization: why it is central for tutoring
Tutoring feedback often depends on *phase-specific events* (e.g., foot strike, takeoff, release, landing). If video, IMU, pressure, and EMG are misaligned, the system can produce *confident but wrong* feedback.

- OpenSense (OpenSim) explicitly assumes sensor fusion and time syncing are already performed before its inverse-kinematics workflow uses IMU orientations [[43]](https://opensimconfluence.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/OpenSim/pages/53084203/OpenSense+-+Kinematics+with+IMU+Data). This means synchronization is a **deployment responsibility**, not a “nice-to-have.”

A concrete synchronization method: a multimodal dataset synchronized radar with motion capture + force plates by using a **linear actuator instrumented to produce a distinctive pattern** at the start/end of recording for alignment across modalities, and calibrated systems to a common global coordinate frame [[33]](https://physionet.org/content/olst-mocap-forceplate-radar/). This illustrates an actionable principle: introduce *shared, detectable sync events* when heterogeneous clocks cannot be perfectly shared.

Industry time-sync approaches (useful when building multi-camera + sensor rigs) commonly cite **Precision Time Protocol (PTP, IEEE 1588)** for sub-millisecond timestamping in multi-sensor setups [[53]](https://things-embedded.com/us/white-paper/synchronizing-machine-vision-with-precision-ieee-1588-ptp/).

### 4.2 Calibration: the make-or-break step for biomechanics-grade feedback

**IMU calibration / sensor-to-segment alignment**
- Joint-angle accuracy depends strongly on calibration method and task; in a sport-specific comparison, absolute mean differences between IMU- and motion-capture-derived ROM ranged from **<0.1° up to 24.1°**, varying by task/joint/calibration [[30]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10534374/).  
- Simulation evidence reinforces that joint-angle estimates can degrade rapidly as relative orientation errors grow [[31]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s12283-024-00483-3).

**OpenSense workflow** provides a practical blueprint:
- Attach IMUs (typically one per tracked segment).  
- Record a known calibration pose matching the model’s default pose; register first timestep to model pose; optionally do heading correction via a base IMU (e.g., pelvis) [[43]](https://opensimconfluence.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/OpenSim/pages/53084203/OpenSense+-+Kinematics+with+IMU+Data).  
- Run inverse kinematics minimizing orientation errors between IMU frames and model segments [[43]](https://opensimconfluence.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/OpenSim/pages/53084203/OpenSense+-+Kinematics+with+IMU+Data).

**EMG calibration/QC**
- Use SENIAM placement, skin preparation, and consistent inter-electrode distances; reliability differs by feature (mean amplitude can be reliable; fatigue/recruitment order may be less so) [[47]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11297106/), [[49]](http://www.seniam.org/).

### 4.3 Labeling/annotation: what you must label for a tutoring system
Tutoring systems usually require labels at multiple levels:

1) **Event labels** (phase boundaries): e.g., foot-lift, stable interval start/end, touchdown—explicitly labeled in synchronized motion-capture/force-plate datasets [[33]](https://physionet.org/content/olst-mocap-forceplate-radar/).  
2) **Action segments**: start/end of skills (temporal localization) [[28]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s10462-024-10934-9).  
3) **Skill quality scores**: overall execution score (AQA) [[74]](https://shunli-wang.github.io/publications/pdf/slwang_AQA-Survey.pdf), [[75]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0957417424025090).  
4) **Fine-grained procedure steps**: step-level labels for sub-actions (e.g., diving phases) used for interpretable, stage-aware assessment [[101]](https://openaccess.thecvf.com/content/CVPR2022/papers/Xu_FineDiving_A_Fine-Grained_Dataset_for_Procedure-Aware_Action_Quality_Assessment_CVPR_2022_paper.pdf), [[102]](https://github.com/xujinglin/FineDiving).  
5) **Context labels** (team sports): player interactions, ball states, formations, camera views—datasets are emerging but annotation is labor intensive [[108]](https://openaccess.thecvf.com/content/CVPR2024W/CVsports/papers/Yeung_AutoSoccerPose_Automated_3D_Posture_Analysis_of_Soccer_Shot_Movements_CVPRW_2024_paper.pdf).

### 4.4 QC in real training
Common QC mechanisms from the sources include:

- **Secure attachment** (IMUs): elastic wrap + athletic tape to reduce skin artifact noise [[30]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10534374/).  
- **Repeat compromised trials** and remove invalid data (e.g., stepping off force plate) [[33]](https://physionet.org/content/olst-mocap-forceplate-radar/).  
- **Bias/representation QC**: demographic imbalance can skew pose accuracy; subgroup testing requires demographic labels and sensitivity checks [[118]](https://ai.sony/blog/Exposing-Limitations-in-Fairness-Evaluations-Human-Pose-Estimation/).

---

## 5) Multimodal fusion strategies and what they’re best for

### 5.1 Canonical fusion taxonomy (with sports-relevant implications)

1) **Early (data/feature-level) fusion**  
   Concatenate features from video/pose/IMU/etc. then learn a joint representation.  
   - Pros: captures interactions across modalities.  
   - Cons: brittle to missing modalities; requires careful normalization/time alignment.

2) **Late (decision-level) fusion**  
   Train separate models per modality; fuse probabilities/scores.  
   - Pros: robust to missing modalities; easier to debug.  
   - Cons: may miss cross-modal synergies.

3) **Hybrid fusion / multi-stream with cross-connections**  
   Combine modality-specific encoders with cross-modal attention/gating; supports partial alignment and differing modality reliability.

A two-stream hybrid example (non-sports but directly analogous to multimodal sensing with sparse anchors): OpenPack’s “ladder-shaped” two-stream network treats sparse high-confidence device events as anchors and continuous sensors as noisy streams, cross-updating in deeper layers [[64]](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2212.11152). This principle maps well to sports: treat coach-tagged events or scoreboard events as anchors, and sensor streams as continuous context.

### 5.2 Attention/transformer-based fusion
Transformers are prominent for aligning long temporal dependencies and cross-modal interactions:

- A “cerebral transformer” integrates **EEG + video** using cross-modal attention to capture neural + movement cues; ablations showed cross-modal attention mattered [[61]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11695414/). While EEG isn’t common in mainstream coaching, the architecture pattern generalizes to fusing any “internal state” modality with external motion.
- AURA-MFM aligns **third-person video, motion capture, IMU, and text** using transformer-based encoders to learn a shared space for zero-shot recognition [[66]](https://arxiv.org/html/2506.03174v1). This is strategically important: it suggests sports S-ITS can use *foundation-model-like pretraining* to reduce labeling burden in new sports/drills.

### 5.3 Graph-based fusion (skeletal structure and multi-agent structure)
Human motion is naturally a graph (joints as nodes). Graph neural networks (often combined with transformers) are used to model joint dependencies:

- A DETR + Graph Convolutional Transformer approach models joints as a graph and uses gating to integrate visual/sensor/temporal info, motivated by occlusion/crowding and the need for real-time processing [[67]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12338817/).  
For coaching: graph models are especially useful for detecting coordination patterns (e.g., knee valgus risk patterns) rather than only keypoint tracking.

### 5.4 Probabilistic/Bayesian fusion (uncertainty and safety)
Probabilistic fusion matters when feedback can cause harm (e.g., recommending aggressive load increases).

- A wearable sensor network fusion architecture for team sports explicitly frames Bayesian inference and Kalman filtering as foundations for combining sources of varying reliability, and reports real-time response times on the order of **192–312 ms** in their system [[62]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-12920-9.pdf).  
- Bayesian modeling is also used for injury-rate estimation with explicit uncertainty handling in soccer surveillance data [[69]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12142926/).  
For S-ITS: Bayesian/uncertainty outputs should be surfaced as “confidence” and used to trigger “ask a human coach / re-capture” behaviors.

### 5.5 Contrastive/self-supervised multimodal learning (label scarcity solution)
Labeling technique errors or quality scores is expensive and often subjective. Self-supervised approaches reduce reliance on labels:

- Multi3Net+ uses **contrastive learning** to align video, pose, and synthetic IMU to transfer knowledge from video to IMU-based recognition [[71]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/computer-science/articles/10.3389/fcomp.2025.1569205/full).  
- A self-supervised contrastive framework for skeleton + IMU learns representations without negatives (VICReg-style) and is motivated by complementary information across modalities [[73]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10354269/).  
- AURA-MFM similarly leverages contrastive alignment for cross-modal generalization [[66]](https://arxiv.org/html/2506.03174v1).

In practice, these methods are strong candidates for **pretraining** on large multi-sport movement corpora, followed by light fine-tuning for a specific team, age group, or drill.

---

## 6) End-to-end S-ITS architecture (reference blueprint)

### 6.1 Functional blocks (what to implement)
**(1) Sensing & session context**
- Cameras (single/multi-view), IMUs, GPS, HR, pressure, EMG, etc., plus drill metadata (what drill, what objective).

**(2) Preprocessing**
- Timestamp alignment; resampling; camera calibration; sensor-to-segment calibration (IMU) [[30]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10534374/), [[43]](https://opensimconfluence.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/OpenSim/pages/53084203/OpenSense+-+Kinematics+with+IMU+Data).  
- QC checks: missing data, sensor detachment, video blur, occlusion severity.

**(3) Per-modality encoders**
- Video → pose/keypoints, optical flow, action features [[28]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s10462-024-10934-9).  
- IMU → temporal embeddings; event detectors; orientation sequences [[43]](https://opensimconfluence.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/OpenSim/pages/53084203/OpenSense+-+Kinematics+with+IMU+Data).  
- Others → specialized embeddings (pressure maps, EMG activation envelopes, gaze features).

**(4) Fusion module**
- Choose hybrid attention/gating for robustness + interpretability; optionally probabilistic fusion for uncertainty [[62]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-12920-9.pdf).

**(5) Task heads**
Typical heads for a tutoring system:
- **Technique**: joint angles, kinematic constraints, error classifiers.  
- **Action segmentation**: detect phases and repetitions [[28]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s10462-024-10934-9).  
- **Action Quality Assessment (AQA)**: predict quality score and stage-wise penalties [[74]](https://shunli-wang.github.io/publications/pdf/slwang_AQA-Survey.pdf), [[75]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0957417424025090).  
- **Workload/risk**: predict overload/injury signals; role/position-specific models (as in rugby GPS workload ML) [[70]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12460712/).

**(6) Learner model**
- Tracks proficiency per skill component, typical errors, responsiveness to feedback, and preference/acceptance.

**(7) Tutoring policy**
- Chooses feedback type/timing/specificity and next drill. Must incorporate evidence that “optimal” feedback schedules are not universal and should be adaptive [[16]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1469029222001455), [[88]](https://www.ovid.com/journals/psyse/pdf/10.1016/j.psychsport.2022.102165~meta-analysis-of-the-reduced-relative-feedback-frequency).

**(8) Feedback delivery**
- Visual overlays (skeleton, angle highlights), side-by-side replay, AR cues, audio cues, haptic cues.  
- Logging for evaluation and auditing.

### 6.2 Edge vs cloud: a practical split
A representative “edge-cloud collaboration” pattern is described in CoachXNet: edge performs low-latency inference, cloud performs richer temporal modeling, then outputs are fused (attention) into an athlete representation and feedback loop [[78]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44196-025-01146-2). Even if you do not replicate that exact design, the principle is useful:

- **Edge**: real-time pose/IMU event detection, immediate safety alerts, privacy filtering.  
- **Cloud**: longitudinal learner modeling, cross-session trend analysis, heavy training jobs, team-level analytics.

A privacy-preserving variant is to do irreversible feature encoding on-device and transmit only abstract vectors to the cloud—proposed in an edge-cloud privacy-preserving behavior recognition architecture [[80]](https://www.arxiv.org/pdf/2601.22938).

### 6.3 Human-in-the-loop workflow (critical in real deployments)
Given coaching staff’s “data analyst” role and their responsibility to balance performance with wellbeing/privacy [[3]](https://learndialogue.org/pdf/MBrewerCHI2025.pdf), S-ITS should support:

- Coach review/override of feedback rules.
- “Why did the system say that?” explanations (feature drivers, comparisons to expert references).
- Controlled data sharing (who sees what, when) aligned with responsible-use guidance [[4]](https://www.ncaa.org/news/2025/12/11/media-center-performance-technology-guidance-approved-by-csmas.aspx).

---

## 7) Tutoring and guidance interventions: what evidence supports (and what remains uncertain)

### 7.1 Augmented feedback (KR/KP) is helpful, but “best schedule” is not settled
A systematic review of augmented feedback for gross motor and sport-specific skills in healthy adults found most studies report learning improvements with augmented feedback, but evidence is conflicting on optimal frequency/timing/duration and many studies have high risk of bias [[16]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1469029222001455).  
A 2022 meta-analysis specifically on reduced relative feedback frequency found **no significant effect** on learning/performance and no acquisition-to-retention reversal, suggesting robust evidence for universal frequency rules is lacking [[88]](https://www.ovid.com/journals/psyse/pdf/10.1016/j.psychsport.2022.102165~meta-analysis-of-the-reduced-relative-feedback-frequency).

**Design implication:** Build S-ITS with *configurable and learnable feedback policies* rather than hard-coding “reduced frequency” as always best [[88]](https://www.ovid.com/journals/psyse/pdf/10.1016/j.psychsport.2022.102165~meta-analysis-of-the-reduced-relative-feedback-frequency), [[16]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1469029222001455).

### 7.2 In PE-like settings, visual feedback matters
A 2022 PE meta-analysis found feedback improved motor skill learning and that **visual** and **visual+verbal** feedback were significant, while **verbal-only** was not significant [[89]](https://www.mdpi.com/1660-4601/19/22/15361).  
A 2022 PE systematic review similarly concluded video-based visual feedback seems effective and often more effective than solely verbal feedback, but must consider deployment constraints and data protection [[90]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s12662-021-00782-y).

**Design implication:** If you must choose modalities under constraints (phones only), prioritize *visual feedback + minimal verbal cues* over voice-only coaching.

### 7.3 Corrective content > evaluative/informative (in PE synthesis)
In the PE meta-analysis, **corrective feedback** was the only feedback content category showing a significant effect in sensitivity analysis [[89]](https://www.mdpi.com/1660-4601/19/22/15361).

**Design implication:** Prefer “change X by Y” feedback over “good/bad” ratings or generic tips—especially for novices.

### 7.4 Autonomy/self-controlled feedback: mixed and likely smaller than commonly claimed
A 2022 meta-analysis on self-controlled motor learning concluded published studies show benefits but selection bias is substantial; adjusted “true” effects were trivially small and not clearly distinguishable from zero [[92]](https://open.lnu.se/index.php/metapsychology/article/view/2803). An experiment also reported choice over feedback schedules was not advantageous [[93]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.3758/s13423-022-02170-5).  
However, a 2025 experiment found **self-controlled timing** of feedback improved retention vs yoked timing and reduced dependency relative to concurrent feedback, with higher intrinsic motivation [[94]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12491054/).

**Design implication:** Don’t treat “autonomy features” as a guaranteed performance booster. Use them to improve *acceptance and engagement*, and test their impact in your sport. If autonomy is used, “timing control” may be more meaningful than arbitrary choices [[94]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12491054/), [[92]](https://open.lnu.se/index.php/metapsychology/article/view/2803).

### 7.5 Attentional focus cues: distal external focus benefits experienced learners
A 2025 systematic review/meta-analysis found distal external focus improved performance vs proximal external focus and internal focus, and skill level moderated effects; distal vs proximal was not significant in novices [[95]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12424610/).

**Design implication:** Generate cues like “send the ball to the back corner” (distal external) for experienced learners; use simpler proximal external cues for novices.

### 7.6 Haptics/vibrotactile feedback in virtual motor learning
A 2022 systematic review reported vibrotactile feedback can effectively aid motor learning in virtual environments and highlighted design considerations (device selection/placement/feedback design) while noting gaps [[97]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/35086007/).

**Design implication:** Haptics is viable, but requires careful placement and encoding; treat it as an engineering + human-factors design problem, not a plug-in.

---

## 8) Applications: concrete system patterns enabled by multimodal fusion

### 8.1 Technique tutoring (single-athlete, movement-quality driven)
**Example outputs**
- Rep detection + phase segmentation  
- Joint angle trajectories (via IMU+biomechanics or video-to-OpenSim pipeline)
- Error type classification (e.g., knee valgus, trunk lean, insufficient depth)

**Why multimodal**
- Video gives global geometry; IMU gives high-rate dynamics; pressure/force reveals loading; EMG reveals activation patterns. Fusion mitigates each modality’s failures [[28]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s10462-024-10934-9), [[30]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10534374/), [[32]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0021929022004286).

**Key constraint**
- Biomechanics-grade validity requires evaluation beyond keypoint accuracy; see OpenCapBench below.

### 8.2 Team-sport tactical tutoring and cognitive guidance
**Pattern:** LLM + visual analytics + scenario playback  
Evidence: ChatGPT + tactical visualization improved knowledge and decision-making and satisfaction vs traditional instruction in a controlled crossover design [[2]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001691825007449).

**Multimodal fusion here**
- Text (explanations) + video/visualization (play context) + structured event logs.

### 8.3 Athlete monitoring, workload, and injury-risk guidance
A rugby ML study used GPS-derived workload metrics and found different best classifiers/predictors by playing position (e.g., random forest vs XGBoost vs SVM depending on subgroup) with F1 up to ~0.66 ± 0.14 [[70]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12460712/).  
A team-sport sensor fusion architecture integrates IMU+GPS+physiology+positioning and reports latency ~192–312 ms [[62]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-12920-9.pdf).

**Design implications**
- Personalization by role/position is not optional; it changes predictors and model choice [[70]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12460712/).  
- Real-time constraints can be met, but must be explicitly engineered and measured [[62]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-12920-9.pdf).

### 8.4 Online PE / remote instruction
A pose-recognition-based AI real-time feedback system evaluated in an 8-week randomized trial improved movement quality and learning-related outcomes in an online course, with increased learning duration as a key mechanism [[119]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s40561-025-00411-3).

**Design implication:** S-ITS can boost outcomes in remote settings, but measurement should include adherence/time-on-task and engagement mechanisms, not just technique scores [[119]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s40561-025-00411-3).

---

## 9) Evaluation and validation (benchmarks + human studies + robustness/fairness)

### 9.1 Why “pose accuracy” metrics are insufficient
OpenCapBench explicitly argues classic pose benchmarks (MPJPE/PCK/mAP) don’t measure **kinematic/physiological correctness**, and provides a benchmark that computes **joint angles via OpenSim** and evaluates RMSE under biomechanical constraints [[110]](https://openaccess.thecvf.com/content/WACV2025/papers/Gozlan_OpenCapBench_A_Benchmark_to_Bridge_Pose_Estimation_and_Biomechanics_WACV_2025_paper.pdf). It reports that common keypoint sets are too sparse for accurate biomechanics and that denser keypoint prediction (via synthetic fine-tuning) can reduce joint angle RMSE substantially [[110]](https://openaccess.thecvf.com/content/WACV2025/papers/Gozlan_OpenCapBench_A_Benchmark_to_Bridge_Pose_Estimation_and_Biomechanics_WACV_2025_paper.pdf).

**Takeaway:** If your S-ITS gives technique advice based on joint mechanics, you must evaluate *joint angle / kinematic errors*, not just 2D keypoint scores.

### 9.2 Action Quality Assessment (AQA): key datasets and metrics (sports)
AQA is central to “tutoring” because it maps to rubric-based scoring and stage penalties.

- FineDiving (CVPR 2022) provides fine-grained step labels and uses **Spearman rank correlation (ρ)** and normalized score error (Relative ℓ2-distance) for evaluation, plus segmentation metrics (AIoU@0.5/0.75) [[101]](https://openaccess.thecvf.com/content/CVPR2022/papers/Xu_FineDiving_A_Fine-Grained_Dataset_for_Procedure-Aware_Action_Quality_Assessment_CVPR_2022_paper.pdf), [[102]](https://github.com/xujinglin/FineDiving).  
- LOGO (CVPR 2023) targets multi-person long-form AQA in artistic swimming (avg ~204 s), adding formation labels and procedure annotations [[106]](https://cvpr.thecvf.com/virtual/2023/poster/22145).  
- LucidAction (NeurIPS 2024 Datasets & Benchmarks) is multi-view, multi-modal (RGB + 2D/3D pose + high-precision MoCap) and curriculum-structured across ability levels, explicitly created to improve generalizability beyond one-shot competition datasets [[107]](https://neurips.cc/virtual/2024/poster/97542).  
- Systematic review evidence indicates sports event scoring is the most common AQA application and **Spearman rank correlation** is widely used (most common metric in a 96-paper review) [[75]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0957417424025090). A broader AQA survey formalizes regression/grading/ranking tasks and metrics (MSE, accuracy, pairwise sorting accuracy, Spearman ρ) [[74]](https://shunli-wang.github.io/publications/pdf/slwang_AQA-Survey.pdf).

### 9.3 Sports pose and sports-context datasets
- AutoSoccerPose introduces 2D pose sequences for soccer shooting in broadcast settings and highlights occlusion/crowding and the labor-intensity of building posture datasets [[108]](https://openaccess.thecvf.com/content/CVPR2024W/CVsports/papers/Yeung_AutoSoccerPose_Automated_3D_Posture_Analysis_of_Soccer_Shot_Movements_CVPRW_2024_paper.pdf).

### 9.4 Robustness testing (domain shift, corruptions, occlusion)
- A corruption robustness benchmark (PoseBench) evaluates pose models under blur/noise/compression/lighting/masks at multiple severity levels, reporting that motion blur and contrast are particularly harmful and that robustness correlates with clean-data performance; it frames this as a safety issue [[116]](https://arxiv.org/html/2406.14367v1). (arXiv—useful but treat as pre-publication.)  
- Occlusion measurement is treated as an explicit evaluation dimension (Occlusion Index evaluated on occlusion-heavy datasets such as OCHuman) [[115]](https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/3747327.3763043).

**Recommendation:** For sports S-ITS, robustness tests should include:
- Motion blur (fast sports), compression artifacts (streaming), harsh lighting (outdoor), occlusions (team sports), and missing modality simulations (IMU dropout).

### 9.5 Fairness across bodies/skin tones/ages (and why it’s hard)
A fairness case study highlights that most pose datasets lack demographic labels; it created COCO-KD with manual labels (gender/age/skin tone) and showed subgroup performance disparities and high sensitivity to sample imbalance; it also warns automated demographic prediction can be highly error-prone and invalidate fairness analyses [[118]](https://ai.sony/blog/Exposing-Limitations-in-Fairness-Evaluations-Human-Pose-Estimation/).

**Implication for sports S-ITS:** If you deploy across diverse athlete populations, you need a fairness evaluation plan with demographic/ability annotation and sensitivity checks, not just aggregate accuracy [[118]](https://ai.sony/blog/Exposing-Limitations-in-Fairness-Evaluations-Human-Pose-Estimation/).

### 9.6 Human outcomes evaluation: retention/transfer, not just immediate performance
Motor learning literature stresses retention as the gold standard for “learning” [[88]](https://www.ovid.com/journals/psyse/pdf/10.1016/j.psychsport.2022.102165~meta-analysis-of-the-reduced-relative-feedback-frequency). Yet many tech prototypes only measure immediate performance.

**Minimum standard for S-ITS evaluations:**
- Pre-test → intervention period → post-test → delayed retention and, if possible, transfer tests.
- Log feedback dosing (frequency/timing/specificity) because multiple reviews note inconsistent reporting hinders conclusions [[16]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1469029222001455), [[96]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11066222/).

---

## 10) Privacy, consent, regulation, and ethics (as of 2026)

### 10.1 Biometric and special-category data (GDPR)
Biometrics used for unique identification are special category data under GDPR; processing is generally prohibited unless explicit consent or another Article 9(2) condition applies [[81]](https://iapp.org/news/a/biometrics-in-the-eu-navigating-the-gdpr-ai-act). Sports tutoring systems often process video-derived body measurements and physiological signals—treat these as sensitive by default.

### 10.2 EU AI Act: biometric categories and restrictions (timeline matters)
An analysis of the EU AI Act’s biometric rules notes regulated categories (remote biometric identification, biometric categorization, emotion recognition) and that some prohibitions took effect in Feb 2025, while high-risk/limited-risk obligations apply from Aug 2026 [[81]](https://iapp.org/news/a/biometrics-in-the-eu-navigating-the-gdpr-ai-act).  
Emotion recognition is especially sensitive; a legal analysis explains emotion recognition is defined as inferring emotions/intentions from biometric data, and clarifies fatigue/pain may not be “emotion” per se, but ambiguity remains and guidance is needed [[82]](https://www.twobirds.com/en/insights/2024/global/what-is-an-emotion-recognition-system-under-the-eus-artificial-intelligence-act-part-1). This matters because sports systems may infer “stress,” “readiness,” or “mental state” from biometrics.

**Practical design posture:** Avoid labeling outputs as “emotion” inference; focus on performance/physiology measures with clear purpose limitation and human oversight.

### 10.3 Sports-governance expectations
- NCAA is developing responsible-use guidance for performance technologies (biometric/performance data devices including cameras/sensors/apps) via consensus processes, indicating governance is moving toward formalized expectations [[4]](https://www.ncaa.org/news/2025/12/11/media-center-performance-technology-guidance-approved-by-csmas.aspx).  
- Elite sport governance emphasizes athlete-centered AI, data integrity, and strict policy; privacy/hacking/manipulation are explicit risks [[5]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11503112/). IOC’s AI Agenda messaging emphasizes equal access and self-determination [[6]](https://stillmed.olympics.com/media/Documents/International-Olympic-Committee/AI/Olympic-AI-Agenda-launch-President-speech.pdf).

### 10.4 Security and privacy-by-design architectures
A privacy-preserving edge-cloud approach proposes doing irreversible encoding at the edge and sending only abstract feature vectors to the cloud for inference [[80]](https://www.arxiv.org/pdf/2601.22938). This pattern is applicable when raw athlete video must not leave the venue/device.

---

## 11) Open problems and failure modes (what will break in practice)

1) **Occlusion and multi-person interactions** (team sports, crowded gyms) remain persistent challenges [[28]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s10462-024-10934-9), [[36]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s10462-025-11344-1), [[108]](https://openaccess.thecvf.com/content/CVPR2024W/CVsports/papers/Yeung_AutoSoccerPose_Automated_3D_Posture_Analysis_of_Soccer_Shot_Movements_CVPRW_2024_paper.pdf).  
2) **Sensor-to-segment calibration** for IMUs is a dominant error source; bad calibration yields misleading joint angles [[30]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10534374/), [[31]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s12283-024-00483-3).  
3) **Label scarcity and subjectivity** for technique errors and “quality” scores; requires either expert labeling or robust self-/weak-supervision [[71]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/computer-science/articles/10.3389/fcomp.2025.1569205/full), [[73]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10354269/).  
4) **Overconfident feedback**: if uncertainty isn’t modeled, the system can confidently coach the wrong correction.  
5) **Feedback dependency / wrong dosing**: evidence for universal feedback-frequency prescriptions is weak; systems that over-coach in real time may harm retention for some tasks [[88]](https://www.ovid.com/journals/psyse/pdf/10.1016/j.psychsport.2022.102165~meta-analysis-of-the-reduced-relative-feedback-frequency), [[94]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12491054/).  
6) **Fairness and representation**: demographic imbalance can induce different pose accuracy across groups; many datasets lack labels to even detect this [[118]](https://ai.sony/blog/Exposing-Limitations-in-Fairness-Evaluations-Human-Pose-Estimation/).  
7) **Regulatory risk** when biometric inference drifts into emotion/intent inference or workplace-like monitoring without appropriate governance [[81]](https://iapp.org/news/a/biometrics-in-the-eu-navigating-the-gdpr-ai-act), [[82]](https://www.twobirds.com/en/insights/2024/global/what-is-an-emotion-recognition-system-under-the-eus-artificial-intelligence-act-part-1).

---

## 12) A practical construction roadmap (what to build first, then expand)

### Phase 1: Single-skill, single-user tutoring with strong validation
- Choose one skill with clear phases (e.g., squat, jump landing, tennis serve segment).  
- Sensors: phone RGB + optional IMU (waist + limb) + optional pressure insole (if foot loading matters).  
- Outputs: rep counting, phase segmentation, 2–3 prioritized error detectors, corrective visual feedback.  
- Validation: biomechanics-grade metrics if you claim biomechanics (OpenCapBench-style joint-angle error evaluation) [[110]](https://openaccess.thecvf.com/content/WACV2025/papers/Gozlan_OpenCapBench_A_Benchmark_to_Bridge_Pose_Estimation_and_Biomechanics_WACV_2025_paper.pdf).

### Phase 2: Multimodal fusion + personalization
- Add hybrid fusion (attention/gating) and uncertainty estimates (Bayesian/Kalman ideas for sensor fusion where applicable) [[62]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-12920-9.pdf).  
- Add learner model that adapts feedback specificity and attentional focus cues (distal external focus for experienced users) [[95]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12424610/).  
- Incorporate logging to study feedback dosing (given evidence uncertainty) [[16]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1469029222001455), [[88]](https://www.ovid.com/journals/psyse/pdf/10.1016/j.psychsport.2022.102165~meta-analysis-of-the-reduced-relative-feedback-frequency).

### Phase 3: Team workflows and tactical tutoring
- Integrate session context, event logs, and explanation modules (language + visual analytics), in line with evidence that language guidance + visualization can improve tactical learning [[2]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001691825007449).  
- Add coach dashboards supporting the data life cycle and controlled sharing to protect athlete wellbeing/privacy [[3]](https://learndialogue.org/pdf/MBrewerCHI2025.pdf).

### Phase 4: Governance-hardening and scalable deployment
- Formalize consent flows, retention/deletion, access control, audit logging, and model monitoring.  
- Align with biometric governance expectations under GDPR/AI Act (EU) and sports-organization guidance trends (e.g., NCAA) [[81]](https://iapp.org/news/a/biometrics-in-the-eu-navigating-the-gdpr-ai-act), [[4]](https://www.ncaa.org/news/2025/12/11/media-center-performance-technology-guidance-approved-by-csmas.aspx).  
- Add fairness evaluation across demographic groups and publish disaggregated performance results where possible [[118]](https://ai.sony/blog/Exposing-Limitations-in-Fairness-Evaluations-Human-Pose-Estimation/).

---

### Bottom line
A sports intelligent tutoring and learning guidance system “driven by multimodal data fusion” is feasible and increasingly evidenced in subcomponents—pose/IMU sensing stacks [[29]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12378739/), [[30]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10534374/), biomechanics-aware evaluation [[110]](https://openaccess.thecvf.com/content/WACV2025/papers/Gozlan_OpenCapBench_A_Benchmark_to_Bridge_Pose_Estimation_and_Biomechanics_WACV_2025_paper.pdf), multimodal fusion models (attention/transformers/graphs/Bayesian) [[61]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11695414/), [[67]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12338817/), [[62]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-12920-9.pdf), and real instructional gains in both tactical tutoring [[2]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001691825007449) and online motor-skill settings [[119]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s40561-025-00411-3). The main determinants of real-world success are **calibration + synchronization**, **biomechanics-appropriate validation**, **adaptive (not rigid) feedback policies given uncertain motor-learning prescriptions** [[88]](https://www.ovid.com/journals/psyse/pdf/10.1016/j.psychsport.2022.102165~meta-analysis-of-the-reduced-relative-feedback-frequency), and **governance/privacy/fairness-by-design** under tightening biometric regulation and sports governance expectations [[81]](https://iapp.org/news/a/biometrics-in-the-eu-navigating-the-gdpr-ai-act), [[5]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11503112/), [[3]](https://learndialogue.org/pdf/MBrewerCHI2025.pdf).
